system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.

This progression is very similar to the last 2, but there are 2 chords in every measure. When this occurs,
you may divide the beats equally (that is, 2 beats for each chord) or unequally (3 beats on the Ist one
and ! beat on the last one; 1 beat on the Ist one and 3 beats on the last one; 3% beats on the Ist one
and % beat on the last one, etc.). If you are playing at a slow tempo, you may find it boring to just
play 1 chord in every measure; however at an extremely fast tempo, you may find it very difficult to
switch to more than | chord per measure, so experiment with the above progression at different
tempos.

This Progression has some interesting f features that were not contained in the others:

a. The F#° in measure 6 — often a #I1V° is used as a passing chord following a IV or IV7 type chord
and then leading to a I or [7 type chord.

This is another type of blues progression with a softer quality that is announced right away with the
use of I# chords. There have been quite a few songs written using large portions of this progression.
Some other points of interest are:

a. The previously mentioned IV to #IV° is here used in the space of only one measure instead of two
as in progression 7.

-b, Am7 in measure 3 can be explained by the synonym Am7 = C6 (See page 12 ).

c. kf and F6 in measure 5 instead of the usual dominant type chords again give the prettier, softer
sound.

d. Fm9 and B7 in measure 6; [Vm is sometimes used following a IV major type chord, and when this
happens, you may treat this [Vm as a IIm, and follow it with its companion V7. In this case, since
the IVm was Fm, if you think of it as the IIm, then the V7 would be B?7,

e. The IIIm7 and/or III7 chord are used often in place of the I chord in measure 7 of blues
progressions. Measure 7 could also have 2 beats of a C or C?#- type chord, and then 2 beats of either
an E7 or Em7 type chord. Examples: C# Em7 or C? E7#9 or C6 E7945, etc.

98



f. Am7 and D9 (measures 8 and 9) similar to progression 6.

g. The last 4 chords in this progression (measures 1] and 12) are called a “turnaround”. A
TURNAROUND IS ANY SERIES OF CHORDS USED IN PLACE OF AN EXTENDED
DURATION OF THE TONIC CHORD. This usually occurs at the end of a phrase or at the end of a
song, although turnarounds are also effective introductions. The last chord of a turnaround is
almost always a V7 type or it 65th (O11).

Confusingly enough, the term “turnaround” is used by rock and 3 chord blues oriented people to
mean the last 4 measures of the 12, but in this book, turnaround will mean the previously stated
definition.

Some of the nearly infinite number of turnarounds are listed here. You may combine ideas from
one turnaround with those of another, if they sound good to you. As usual, apply the substitution
principles to these turnarounds if you wish, because they may sound boring if they are played straight
as 7th chords, m7th chords, etc.

Points worth noting in this progression:

a. Measures 3 and 4 are actually a turnaround leading to the F chord(s). This can be thought of as VI
IL V 1 of C or Il VE Il V of F. The first 3 of these chords could also have been of the m7 family,
but the last one should be of the dominant family to lead the ear to the F chord.

b. Measure 8(E°m? A>7), This can also be looked at in different ways — either the bitIm7 and its
companion dominant chord (see progression 8) being used in between the IIIm7 (Em7) and Ilm7
(Dm7) for a chromatic effect (chromatic means going in one direction and not skipping any notes.
Example: A chromatic scale going up D D# E F F* etc.; a chromatic scale going down BbAG*G
F# etc.) or the bym7 of Am7, which normally would be i is between the Em7 and Dm7.

c. Note the Dm755s and remember they are also Fm6 and Bb9 — see synonyms.

Points of interest:

a. Measure 4 — since you may substitute GO7 (F#7) for C7 due to the >V principle, sometimes you
may put its companion m7 before it where time and your ears permit. Remember that the
companion chords are thought of as IIm7 and V7.

b. Measure 3 — these chords are not easy, but with practice...

c. Measure 6 — B13 is used without the Fm7 as in progression 8.

d. Measure 7 — I to IV7 is a common change in this measure, and major type and dominant type
chords could be mixed on the I and/or IV (like F¥, IV or I7, IV9 or 19, IV6, etc.).

e. Me 8 — the reason Gm6 is substituted for Em7 is because Em7%5 can be used for Em? and
Bat, '5 IS Gm6 (see Synonyms). Think about the implications here— like maybe Gm9 or Gm11 for

m7, etc.

f. Measure 9 — notice the triads on the top 3 strings. These chords could be thought of as D7 chords
with B® and B triads superimposed on top; this type of chord is called a Polytonal or bi-tonal chord
as you have encountered earlier in section 6.

g. Measure 12 — F6/9 for Dm7. Notice that F6/9 — Dm7/11.

This progression is more of a “solo” progression due to the abundance of high voicings (that is, on
the high strings instead of the lower strings). Many of these chords would be too shallow sounding for
thythm playing.

Points of interest:

a. There are 4 chords in some measures and even 5 and 6 chords in measures 9 and 10. You must feel
how to put them in; there are no rules for it. However, don’t try to play this progression at too fast
a tempo or it will be extremely difficult to play as many as 6 chords in one measure; it will work
nicely at a moderate tempo though.

b. Measure 3 — (Em7) the II[m7 sometimes follows a I chord in this measure.

. Measures 5 and 6 — closer voicings in these chords — closer to a “piano” sound.

. Measure 8 — the E7#9 is used as a temporary passing chord between the other A7 types. Using the

V7 type of chord instead of the usual Vm7 is sometimes done.

e. Measure 10 — the A759 is used as a passing chord here in the same fashion as the E7#9 in measure

8. Again, it is the V7 of the chords it goes in between.

Points of interest:

a. G7#9 in measure 2 — this is the V7 being used as a temporary passing chord, just as in the previous
“major blues” progressions. All the blues progressions that you have encountered until now are
often referred to as “major bhues” to distinguish them from blues progressions that are based on
minor chords.

b. Ab® Gm7, Fm7 chords in measures 5 and 6 — this is the widely used concept of playing a fragment

of the fundamental harmonies of a scale. When this is done, the starting chord is not that important,

but the ending chord is very important. It must fit with the chord that the fragment is being

substituted for; it also must lead nicely to the next chords to be used. In the above case, the A

Gm7 Fm7 is a fragment of the dorian minor scale (see page 89 ), and since the Fm7 is the last chord
of the fragment and the basic chord above it is Fm, there is no trouble here. One other word of
caution — when using substitute chords from different minor scales, if you are playing by yourself,
